input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The MABI MAX 100 industrial robot, a six-axis articulated system with specific focus on its first vertical axis. Key components include a motor, gearboxes, and a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl control system. Operational domain involves precision machining tasks with defined reach capabilities, characterized by high mechanical complexity and real-time control requirements, making it suitable for digital twinning to enable predictive maintenance and dynamic performance optimization.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides real-time identification of white-box and black-box friction models for the robot's first vertical axis, enabling precise motion control. It includes dashboard visualization for monitoring system states and semantic support to clarify physical relationships and system understanding, directly contributing to improved motion accuracy in machining operations.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Tooling and enablers encompass Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for multi-level abstraction modeling (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer), methodologies including RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT for complexity handling, architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs for component-and-connector-based system descriptions, and integration with simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink, general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java), and other simulation methods for detailed behavior specification.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Structural models using Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as SysML IBDs, AADL, and MontiArc to define components, interfaces, and interconnections; behavioral models implemented via state machines in SysML v1.7 and MontiArc Automaton, supplemented by simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose code (C++/Java). Data originates from physical system states via one-way data flow, with management addressing heterogeneous stakeholder integration across abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer).
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series sensor data and operational data from physical assets are streamed via MQTT protocol for real-time data transmission, enabling live-data linking and integration of live measurements from the robot.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle encompasses development phase using Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) with SysML for requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer modeling, transitioning to operational phase where automated data flow from physical systems (e.g., robot motion sensors) to the digital twin enables real-time monitoring and predictive analysis.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows a structured methodology including problem identification, asset and model analysis, and use or creation of calculations/models, with validation against physical system data and iterative evolution managed through model updates based on operational feedback.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the system as the 'MABI MAX 100' and correctly specifies the 'Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl' control system and the focus on the 'first vertical robot axis'. It correctly lists components like the motor and gearboxes and mentions the machining domain and reach capabilities. However, it slightly deviates by stating the system is suitable for twinning to enable 'predictive maintenance,' which is not mentioned in the source text; the text focuses on 'improve robot’s motion accuracy' and friction modeling. Supported by quotes: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes', 'first vertical robot axis at the base is used for creating a digital shadow', 'The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl'.

The extraction accurately describes the Digital Twin's services, including real-time identification of white-box and black-box friction models, dashboard visualization for monitoring, and semantic support for understanding physical relationships. These points are directly supported by the text, which mentions the integration of analytical and data-driven friction models, live-data linking, dashboard visualization, and semantic support. The extraction also correctly notes the contribution to improved motion accuracy. The description is specific, technically relevant, and well-supported. 

Relevant quotes:
1. 'Our methodology involves modeling the industrial robot’s mechanical structure, incorporating friction-related behavior, implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS), and live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard' (Abstract)
2. 'The resulting digital shadows enable the continuous, multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction, using both white-box and black-box models to enhance the robot’s motion accuracy' (Abstract)
3. 'Linking of the data with the model should thus lead to semantic support and ensure that the overall system can be better understood' (Section I)

The extraction is highly detailed and matches the source text accurately. Section II.A lists Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) using SysML, methodologies RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT, and ADLs like ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs. It also mentions integration with MathWorks Simulink and general-purpose languages like C++ and Java. The extraction comprehensively captures these technical tools and enablers described in the background section.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The document mentions 'real-time' data and live measurements but does not provide specific technical details regarding synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, time scales for different services, or temporal granularity needs as defined in the characteristic description. Quote: 'real-time, data-driven representations of robot joint behavior'.

The paper describes the creation of a single digital shadow for the first axis of a single industrial robot. It does not discuss multiple digital twin instances, hierarchical structures of instances, centralized vs decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between multiple twins. Quote: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100... The first axis is used for creating a digital shadow.'

While the document presents a reference model and component integration for a digital shadow, it explicitly distinguishes the shadow as a passive entity that lacks the active orchestration, resource management, and system-wide coordination mechanisms implied by a Digital Twin constellation. Quote: 'a digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system'.

The text mentions Asset Administration Shells (AAS) and MQTT for linking the robot to the shadow, but it does not describe integration with external enterprise systems, interoperability patterns with other Digital Twins, or system-wide dependencies beyond the specific physical asset. Quote: 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS), and live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard.'
